name: Monozukuri Culture in Japanese Society
summary_excerpt: 【TAISI Course】 “Monozukuri” (literally "making things") is a key idea th......
subtitle: "Monozukuri" in Japanese Companies and Society
summary:
【TAISI Course】
“Monozukuri” (literally "making things") is a key idea that characterizes craftsmanship and technological development in Japan.  It also has been one
of the main factors that lead to the success of Japanese products in the postwar period.  However, since the fall of the Bubble Economy in 1990s, a simple emphasis on technology no longer translates into a formula for profit or survival, and many
companies and individuals have worked towards adjusting the monozukuri tradition for a new age.
【In-Person Course】
The course will be taught in classroom only.
goal: The primary reading material for this class is "Made in Japan" by Morita Akio, one of the illustrious founders of Sony, who himself became a symbol of "Japan Inc." Morita's book was originally written and published in English as a manifesto of the Japanese way of business, and, even after 30 years, its arguments and issues are still interesting and relevant. We will study the classical elements of monozukuri with "Made in Japan"; the course will also look into the historical traditions and contemporary aspects.
study_outside: For each class students are expected to spend 1 to 2 hours to read required texts and prepare for assignments.

name: Women in Modern Japan: Outside Encounters and Early Changes
teacher: ホートン　ウィリアム　ブラッドリー
summary_excerpt: 【TAISI Course】 This course will examine the images and status of women f......
summary:
【TAISI Course】
This course will examine the images and status of women from the mid-19th century through the second half of the 20th century. Prior to World War II, both prostitutes and the western import of a “good wives, wise mothers” model were particularly noteworthy. Discussion of the postwar period will include women’s suffrage, women’s (equal) rights, and the expansion of education for women encouraged by GHQ, and the changing roles of mothers in society.
In addition to short lectures, we will discuss readings and other materials every week. Generally, we will work in groups, and each group will present once—groups will be shuffled after the first 5 presentations and new groups will be responsible for another presentation. Following the presentations we will have group discussions, then a final wrap-up discussion together.
Readings and the schedule may change.
goal: Students will gain a knowledge of the representations of Japanese women and some of the contexts in which these women found themselves during the first 100 years following the Meiji Restoration of 1868 and be able to utilize this to contextualize and better understand different women in contemporary Japan.
study_outside: Students are expected to read assigned readings before class and bring paper copies of the readings to class. Use of smart phones, tablets and computers is strongly discouraged except in special circumstances.
schedule_plan:
1:
第１回　Introduction
Introduction to the course including the course requirements and readings.
2:
第２回　Japanese Women According to Basil Hall Chamberlain (19th Century)
Discussion of the ideas about Japanese Women during the 19th Century, especially the observations of Basil Hall Chamberlain. Includes discussion of Onna Daigaku.
Reading: Basil Hall Chamberlain,
Things Japanese
(title varies)
3:
第３回　Meiji Era Women and the Role of the Elite
Discussion of Meiji Era women and the role of the elite in disseminating ideologies about women and gender.
Reading: Sally Hastings, “A Dinner Party is not a Revolution”
4:
第４回　Meiji Era Women: An Alternative View
A second discussion of Meiji Era Women.
Reading: Sharon Nolte and Sally Hastings, “The Meiji State’s Policy toward Women, 1890-1910”
5:
第５回　Taisho and Early Showa Era Women
A discussion about Taisho and Early Showa Era Women. Reading: Margit Nagy, “Middle Class Working Women during the Interwar Years”

name: Culture, Nation, and Modernity
summary_excerpt: This course explores how "culture," "nation," and "modernity" have develop......
summary:
This course explores how "culture," "nation," and "modernity" have developed as intertwined
concepts, rethinking the basic terms and concepts in the study of culture from a critical perspective of cultural and social theories. It highlights the significance of interdisciplinary contextual analysis, discussing the way in which "language," "theatre," and "rituals" had been represented and practiced within the specific historical and social contexts of Japanese modernization and nation-building efforts.
It also points to the complexity of the concepts by looking into them in terms of the issues of identity, politics, and power.
goal: critical reexamination of key terms and concepts of studying culture, from interdisciplinary, holistic, multiple perspectives

name: On Identity: A Socio-political Analysis
summary_excerpt: 【CJSP/TAISI Course】 During the last thirty years, issues of identity h......
summary:
【CJSP/TAISI Course】
During the last thirty years, issues of identity have been unavoidable in discussions of nationalism and gender studies. Who is Japanese? Do these individuals identify themselves as a member of the Japanese community? Are Korean-Japanese identified as Japanese or/and Korean? By who? How should women or men behave? Although they are easy questions at first glance, questions about who is who are still important subjects, particularly in the current global societies with their diversities. In this class I will introduce new terms such as multiple identities and drifting identities to help students understand other socio-political values. On the one hand, I will touch on identity formation, development,cultural identity, identity politics and so on, and on the other hand we will discuss these processes related to Japanese cultural history.
goal: The goal of this course is to understand theories on identity and their application to Japanese social situations. The theoretical understanding with concrete applications will help students explore other area studies as well.　Students without prior experience in Japanese studies will find the course difficult in the beginning, but will obtain a payoff in deep knowledge by the end of this course. This course will help students to move on to further advanced studies in TAISI courses.
study_outside: Discussion is one of the most important tools to understand theories in this course. Students are expected to read weekly reading materials before the class. After the class, self-review of the class with your brief description is expected for accumulating theoretical knowledge which is introduced in this class.
schedule_plan:
1:
第１回　Introduction
The first meeting will be a class orientation which includes detailed explanation of the required course work, the grading basis, self-introductions, etc.
This class will be organized with the first half lecture and the second half student activities (group discussion and presentation)
2:
第２回 The Process of Forcible Identification　I
We will watch the film "One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest" during the 2nd and the 3rd class meetings. In this class, we will watch the half of the film. While watching this, students have to focus on how segregation and discourse would change individual identity. We will discuss these in the second half of this class.
3:
第３回　The Process of Forcible Identification II
We will continue watching the film. The second half of this class will be a discussion of how identity has been forcibly molded.
.
4:
第４回　Nationalism and Identity I
According to Euro-American scholars, Japan's nationalism emerged in the mid-19th century.  In this class, I will lecture on the different devices for creating imagined communities, as well as the individuals who would be members of the community.
Required reading: "Official Nationalism" in Imagined Communities (Benedict Anderson). We will have a discussion on this material in the second half of this class.
5:
第５回　Natioalism and Identitiy II
From the Meiji period, Japan tried to expand and modernize to pursue the power of western societies. Throughout its expansionist period, Japan colonized neighboring countries and territories. This class will discuss the identification and identities of the new citizens.
Reading requirement: "Citizenship" in Re-inventing Japan
6:
第６回　Minorities and Identities
During the last couple of years, the slogan of "Black lives matter" has been presented through media and on the street. Some people say that this is not only a problem of blacks but also more generally a minority issue. In this class I will lecture, providing a brief history of Japanese minorities. Based on the lecture and reading materials, students will discuss minorities in Japan and identities.
7:
第７回　Midterm Exam and Lecture on Gender I
The 60-minute midterm exam will cover up to week 6 materials.
After the exam, I will lecture on the history of women's studies.
8:
第8回 Gender II
Lecture on the genealogy of gender and history in Japan. We will have a discussion on this material in the second half of this class.
Reading material is an article by Galye Rubin
9:
第９回　The Problems of Gender Identities
This class will discuss the problems of gender identification, and specifically its stereotype.
We will have a group discussion and presentation on assigned reading materials, Rubin and Mohanty.
Reading material: "Under Western Eyes" by Chandra Mohanty
10:
第１０回　Gender Trouble
Brief lecture on Foucault and Butler and group discussion and presentation on Butler.
Reading materials: Gender Trouble chapter 1 (Judith Butler)
11:
第１１回　Think about Gender Trouble through Oneman (Transgender TV celebrities)
This class will discuss the recent popularity of transgendered TV celebrities and their function in considering gender discourses in Japan.
12:
第１２回　Creating Identities through Repetitious Practices
We will watch the film "Trading Places". While watching this film, students have to focus on how positions, public expectations and repetitious practices form individual identity.
13:
第１３回 Creating Identities
This class will be used for group presentations on formation of identities.
14:
第１４回 　Beyond "Identity"
Wrap-up and submission of the final paper
textbook: Butler, Judith (1990) Gender Trouble: Feminism and the Subversion of Identity, Routlege.

name: Environmental Law in Japan
summary_excerpt: This course provides an overview of the rapidly developing field of domest......
summary: This course provides an overview of the rapidly developing field of domestic environmental law. It examines the fundamental principles of environmental law such as sustainable development, polluter -pays principle, precautionary principles and environmental rights, which appear in concrete statutes. It will consider the clean air act, waste control & recycling laws, chemical regulations laws, climate & energy laws, nature & ecosystem protection laws, and so on. Students are required to prepare for the class for more than one hour beforehand.
goal: At the conclusion of this course, students should be able to show familiarlity with fundamental terminology of environmental law, and demonstrate an understanding of the core component of environmental law.
study_outside: Please prepare each class with reading materials assigned in the class.
schedule_plan:
1:
第１回 Introduction
An introduction of “the environmental law” course in Japan, focusing on history of pollution problems.
2:
第２回 Sustainable Development
Sustainable Development is the most fundamental principle in the environmental law. It apperares in the Environmental Policy Act of 1993 and other statutes.
SDGs extended the sustainable development. It now consists of three pillows, i.e., environment, society and ecomomy.
3:
第３回 basic mechanism of environmental regulation
The command & control regulatory scheme has been successful to prevent pollutions in Japan.
As the urban environment deterioration came to attract the attention of people, the regulation using market mechanism became popular. The economic methods and the information method are popular end effective.
4:
第４回 Nature Protection
Nature Parks system with 34 national parks plays an important role in the protection of nature.
There are many other nature reserve schemes, such as the natural environment conservation areas and the birds and animals protection areas.
Priviously the beautiful natural scene was protected, but now, the ecosystems or biodiversity is protected.
5:
第５回 Socio-ecological productive landscape
Most of the nature in Japan is not a pristin nature. They are half-manmade, half natural ones, i.e., socio- ecological productive landscape, "SATOYAMA."
To protect the SATOYAMA environment, encouraging farming and forestry is important.
6:
第６回 air pollution and water pollution
Let's study the outline of the regulatory system, the measures taken at factories and workplaces. then  automobile emission regulations under the Air Pollution Control Law.
Then  let's study the process of enacting the Water Pollution Control Act. After reviewing the outline of the regulations in the Water Pollution Control Act, students learn about measures to be taken measures at factories and workplaces.
7:
第７回 Green Space
Ecosystem in a city is essencial for people live happy. You will study urban planning scheme and urban green space scheme.
8:
第８回 Polluter-Pays Principle
The polluter-pays principle requires for the polluter to pay the cost of pollution.
The principle was first recommended by OECD in 1970s.
Which cost should be paid by the polluter is controversial.
9:
第９回 waste management & recycling
It examines the Waste Management and Public Cleansing Act in Japan. It reviews the enactment process of the waste disposal act in Japan, then, the outline of the regulatory system Students will then examine the businesses, facilities, and responsibilities for handling general and industrial wastes sites. The Waste Disposal and Public Cleansing Act sets standards for the proper disposal of waste, for treatment, and for maintenance and management, recording duty which allows residents of the surrounding area to access.
10:
第１０回 environmental rights
IIt considers the right to live in a health environmental and the rights to access nature.
Now, the procedural environmental right is getting important, but the substantial environment is still important.
11:
第１１回 Precautionary Principle
In order to protect the environment, the precautionary approach shall be widely applied by States according to their capabilities. Where there are threats of serious or irreversible damage, lack of full scientific certainty shall not be used as a reason for postponing cost-effective measures to prevent environmental degradation. (Rio declaration, principle 15)
The regulation of chemical substances and soil contamination will be studied.
12:
第１２回 Climate Change
Let's study climate law in Japan which intends to reduce GHG emission.
The nuclear energy safety regulation and the renewables energy penetration will be studied.
13:
第13回　Environmental Impact assessment
EIA is the inevitable tool to realize ecologically sustainable development.
EIA is the process for information disclosure and public involvement.
14:
第１４回 term-end exam
Term-end examination in the classroom.

summary_excerpt: 【TAISI Course】On-demand instructions are provided for this course. ......
summary:
【TAISI Course】On-demand instructions are provided for this course.
Japan has gone through drastic and upward changes in economic activities with various challenges. Environmental degradation in the1960s, energy crisis in the 1970s, trade imbalance in the 1980s, deflation in the 1990s and fiscal deficit in the 2000s are the major challenges which the Japanese economy and society have encountered. This course is offered to understand the causes and the solutions to these problems. This intellectual exercise would give positive and negative lessons to the countries which are facing similar challenges.
Macroeconomic theory is studied first to understand the determinants for output and roles of fiscal and monetary policies. The theory is then applied to the data from the Japanese economy.
goal: The main objective of this course is to learn the basic tools of macroeconomics and to understand the challenges faced by the Japanese economy is another objective.

This course seeks to deepen our understanding of postwar relations between Japan and the U.S.  Students are required to read the assigned material(s) carefully every week and follow the instructions for homework, group discussions, presentations, and peer commenting. After the registration period, a very detailed “Weekly Schedule" will be distributed to the students via Waseda Moodle. Additional information and instruction for each class will also be posted on Waseda Moodle.
Please note that course activities are held online, and this course is likely to be part of the Collaborative Online International Learning (COIL) program which connects students internationally online. The COIL for this course aims to enhance intercultural interaction among students in Japan and the United States; therefore, several classes will be allocated for the discussions across the pacific. Because of the time difference, the activities are fully held online at mutually convenient timing for students. To make such an arrangement possible, an online communication tool (e.g., Padlet) will be used with the overseas university. For this particular purpose, please understand in advance that we will provide your Waseda email address to the overseas university's office to register for the service. Your email address will be used only for the registration purpose to make the online communication tool available for you and will not be disclosed to students of the overseas university without your consent. The details of the arrangement will be announced later as the international arrangement develops gradually. Also, the “Weekly Schedule” will be adjusted accordingly.
Finally, the class size of this course is limited to 20 students at maximum.
goal: Students will deepen their understanding of the US-Japan relationship and will be introduced to the major themes and issues which both underpin and, at times, strain the alliance.
study_outside: A very detailed "Weekly Schedule" will be provided in class. Each week, students will have assigned reading materials which must be read before the class. Students must complete and submit all the assignments on time.

name: Contemporary Social Affairs 2
teacher: ヤン　マニュエル　ミナミ
summary_excerpt: 【TAISI Course】 We all live and die in capitalist society. Capitalism ......
subtitle: Capitalism, Poverty, and Class Struggle
summary:
【TAISI Course】
We all live and die in capitalist society. Capitalism seems like such an inescapable part of reality that we cannot imagine any other society. It weighs on us as if it were fate itself. Study hard, get into a good school, and land a decent job. Work hard. Make a lot of money. Even if you can't make a lot, at least make enough to buy things and support your family like the average Joe. If you can't, you are a loser. You’re responsible for your conditions. If you can't take responsibility, you deserve to be eliminated from society. Such dog-eat-dog worldview has been ingrained in us from an early age through media, education, and family. We may hear fine-sounding sentiments like “don't pick on the weak," “helping others is important," and “all people are equal.” But it is obvious from the daily news that these are nothing but lies. If you have money, you are treated well and given a free pass; if you don't, you are ridiculed and exploited. This is what Shakespeare said about the power of money:
Thus much of this will make black white, foul fair,
Wrong right, base noble, old young, coward valiant.
Capitalist society is driven by money, which has the overwhelming power to turn reality upside down. In this class, we will trace the origins, structure, and future of this strange society through various movies and readings. Why do economic crashes and depressions, which shake the world economy, occur?
How do poor children and elderly live? What does the value of labor-power mean and what is class struggle?  By discussing such concrete social issues in depth, we will explore the possibility of society that can replace capitalism.
goal: For class participants to understand the nature of contemporary capitalism, relate their relevant experiences, and share their thoughts both in discussion and writing.
study_outside: Every two weeks, a reading/visual material will be assigned to you, along with a set of questions to which you will write a response.

name: Introduction to Social Research A
summary_excerpt: This course provides essential knowledge for conducting research in the so......
summary: This course provides essential knowledge for conducting research in the social sciences. It includes an overview of the historical background of natural science and the differences between natural and social sciences. The course will primarily emphasize causal inference as scholars increasingly focus on understanding causality rather than mere correlation. Although it will not cover data handling or statistical software, it will showcase exemplary studies that effectively apply causal inference.
goal: Students will understand the difference between natural science and social science and the distinction between normative and empirical studies. They will also learn the importance of causal inference in social science.
study_outside: The lecturer will upload slides before each lecture, and it is recommended that students review them in advance. A wide range of references will also be provided, and students are encouraged to consult them.

name: Evolutionary Anthropology
summary_excerpt: 【TAISI Course】 In this course, students learn physical anthropology and ......
summary:
【TAISI Course】
In this course, students learn physical anthropology and evolutionary models to understand humans from the viewpoint of evolutionary biology. Specifically, they learn the basics of population genetics, evolutionary game theory, sexual selection, cultural evolution, and gene-culture coevolution, as well as human evolution. In each lecture, they study calculation methods of evolutionary models and solve exercises. They also have to review the lecture by homework. Double registration of this course and Theoretical Anthropology 2 (Japanese course) is not allowed.
goal: To know the concepts of evolutionary models, to master their calculation methods, to improve logical thinking skills, and to deepen the understanding of evolution and humans.
study_outside: Preparation is unnecessary, but homework is given (around 90 minutes). Math is heavily used in this course, so those who are not good at math must learn it by themselves (if you do not master high school math well, it may be difficult to follow this course).

name: Social Business
summary_excerpt: We face various problems, such as poverty, inequality, discrimination, cli......
summary: We face various problems, such as poverty, inequality, discrimination, climate change, etc. These problems threaten our lives. How can we solve them and make our society sustainable? One approach to challenging such problems is through businesses—social business. Businesses support the current capitalist economic system, while social businesses pursue dual missions of social and economic goals. There is a growing expectation for social businesses to address social problems to realise a sustainable society. The concept of social business is not new and has historically existed in many countries. In the last decades, however, it has been popular and is becoming a trend among those who have dreams/passions to change society. This introductory course provides the background and overviews of social businesses and explores several social business cases with a particular focus on Japan. It helps understand social businesses, which challenge changing people's behaviours towards the realisation of a sustainable society.
study_outside: It is essential that students do the required preparatory reading for in-class discussions. Students are expected to carefully read and study the materials at home. It is strongly recommended that students take notes when they read them. Note-taking is not simply a means to record information, but it is also an important part of your learning. It is also useful for students to pay attention to media presentations of social problems and topics related to social businesses.

name: Social Business Practicum
summary_excerpt: In recent decades, it has been recognised that the business approach is ef......
summary: In recent decades, it has been recognised that the business approach is effective in addressing social problems facing our globalising society. Japan, as well as other countries, is currently struggling with social problems caused by depopulation, ageing, gender inequalities, unemployment, natural disasters, and so on. Some people turn to social business as a means to contribute to solving these issues. Prior to planning social businesses, however, it is imperative to understand socio-economic circumstances and identify problems that prospective social entrepreneurs want to solve/mitigate for the betterment of society. This course provides students with opportunities to learn how to explore and identify problems by using qualitative research methods. This course also introduces several research methods and challenges in diverse applied contexts.
goal:
Through this course, students will be able to:
- understand qualitative research methods and various applied fields;
- develop analytic and problem-solving abilities in practice; and
- improve written and oral communication skills to explain the findings based on qualitative research.
study_outside: It is essential that students do the required preparatory reading for in-class discussions and assigned tasks. It is strongly recommended that students carefully read the reading materials at home and take notes. Note-taking is not simply a means to record information, but it is also an important part of your learning. Students are also expected to actively participate in in-class activities and conduct their own research outside the classroom.

A healthy debate is crucial to identifying a social solution in a democracy!

https://www.chathamhouse.org/about-us/chatham-house-rule#:~:text=The%20Rule%20reads%20as%20follows,other%20participant%2C%20may%20be%20revealed
, accessed on January 30, 2024

* In case of any opportunity to learn social problems, your class schedule may be adjusted to learn alternatively.

summary_excerpt: 【TAISI Course】 In an age where organizations straddle and traverse nat......
subtitle: Introduction to cross-cultural organizational behavior
summary:
【TAISI Course】
In an age where organizations straddle and traverse national boundaries with considerable ease, future managers aspiring to work in the international arena need to familiarize themselves with the international dimensions of management disciplines such as organizational behavior. This course seeks to expose students to a broader, global perspective on organizational behavior and business management. The course is composed of various topics related to human behavior in organization; personality and values, emotions and moods, perceptions and decision making, and organizational culture.
*
This course will be offered in the fall
quarter
and will meet every Wednesday
during 2nd and 3rd periods for seven weeks.

name: Development Studies
summary_excerpt: This course provides students with the opportunity to gain a solid underst......
summary: This course provides students with the opportunity to gain a solid understanding of development studies from both theoretical and empirical perspectives. By critically examining a wide range of approaches to development, students will identify challenges and opportunities in contemporary development efforts and explore innovative ways to bring about positive change in today's world. The course uses a reflective learning approach along with a variety of active learning methods. Rather than treating development as a neutral or technical process, the course pays particular attention to power, inequality, and the contested meanings of ‘development’ across different historical and political contexts.
goal: By the end of this course, students will be able to:
study_outside:
Pre-class Learning
Students are required to complete the assigned reading before each class. The readings are intended to provide a basis for discussion rather than a set of conclusions to be mastered. Students are encouraged to pay attention to how labour is described and interpreted in the text, and to identify questions, assumptions, or tensions that emerge from the reading.
Post-class Learning
Following each class, students should take time to reflect on the themes discussed and the questions raised. Instead of aiming for definitive answers, students are encouraged to consider the limits of their understanding and the perspectives they encountered. These reflections will support deeper engagement with the course and contribute to the final written assignment.

【
TAISI Course
】
This course discusses conflict analysis and resolution theory, and interactive approaches before/during/after conflict. We explore diagnosing conflict, influences and context, and intervening in the conflict. In class, we start with a digesting time (15 minutes), a lecture (60 minutes) and a discussion (25 minutes) to apply a theory to a real-world conflict.
goal: Students understand conflict analysis and resolution theory and scientifically apply it to real-time conflict and its resolution.
study_outside: Please read the assigned chapter of the textbook before the class. For the class ‘Peacebuilding’, your reading materials will be distributed before the class.

name: The Inside of Peacebuilding
summary_excerpt: * Please read the Remarks section before you register, especially "A Matri......
summary:
*
Please read the Remarks section before you register,
especially "A Matrix of Class Indicators on the English Language Requirement Levels and Theoretical Comprehensive Levels."
【
TAISI Course
】
This course explores third-party support, which creates dynamism in peacebuilding. We actively listen to our guest speakers about the challenges and strategies for intervention by the international community, especially JICA, the Ministry of Foreign Affairs, the Cabinet Office, INGOs, UN agencies, universities, and/or foreign academia. Following the lectures, we apply the practical experiences to conduct role-playing, focusing on funding, cooperative relationships, and implementation.  Lastly, you will present your project plan.
In addition, this is an opportunity to peek into your future job in international cooperation.
Previously, I worked at the Atlantic Council in Washington, DC (think tank), as a secretary to a former Japanese parliament member (former Parliamentary Vice-Minister of Foreign Affairs), the United Nations University hq, and the Secretariat of the International Peace Cooperation hq. at the Cabinet Office of Japan, Japanese PKO missions in post-conflict countries, Hiroshima University, and JICA peacebuilding project implementation.
goal: Practically, students learn the role of third-party support in peacebuilding, its dynamism and its reality.
study_outside: Reading a paper and/or an article will be assigned to each class. In addition, some practical research will be required before the role-playing and presentation.
schedule_plan:
1. The orientation:
2. The introduction: Various policy frameworks and third-party approaches to peacebuilding will be discussed. Additionally, it will introduce practical issues for mission preparation and training.
3-8. A guest lecture: Each lecture will be delivered by an officer (or former officer) from JICA, the MOFA, the Cabinet Office, INGOs, UN agencies, and/or universities (in random order). The speaker is subject to change based on their availability.
9-11. Role-playing: I provide a scenario. Firstly, we analyze conflict, actors and its roles in peacebuilding.  Secondly, we conduct role-playing, especially in funding, cooperative relationships, and implementation.
12-13. Presentations
14. Summary and Wrap-up

name: Organizational Innovation １
summary_excerpt: Drawing from multiple social science disciplines such as management (espec......
summary: Drawing from multiple social science disciplines such as management (especially strategic management and organization theory), sociology, and political science, this course aims to expose students to various types of organizational innovation at different levels, i.e., firm, interfirm, and interactions of firms and other stakeholders as well as external environments. By discussing relevant materials and cases, students will learn challenges and opportunities in organizational innovation.
goal: By the end of the course, students will be able to:
study_outside: Course materials in Power Points will be uploaded in advance. Students are required to read the materials carefully before they come to class.
schedule_plan:
1:
第１回: Course introduction
Introduction of course contents and assessment methods. Discussion: what is organizational innovation?
2:
第２回: Strategic Management and Firm Innovation 1
We discuss the impact of industry environment on firm strategy and innovation.  Basic frameworks to analyze the industry environment are introduced,
3:
第３回: Strategic Management and Firm Innovation 2
We discuss the effects of macro environments (i.e., PESTEL) on firm strategy and innovation.
4:
第４回: Strategic Management and Firm Innovation 3
This session covers firms' internal resources and how those resources affect firm innovation based on resource-based view.
5:
第５回: Different Levels of Strategy
We discuss business-level and corporate-level strategies and the relationships between those strategies and firm innovation.
6:
第６回: Strategic Alliances and Firm Innpvation
This session covers how firms can facilitate their innovation using strategic alliances. We discuss pros and cons of strategic alliances.
7:
第７回: M&A and Firm Innovation
We discuss how M&A can be an effective tool to enhance firm innovation.  The session explores advantages and disadvantages of M&A.
8:
第８回: Corporate Governance and Firm Innovation
We discuss how corporate governance practices such as board of directors, ownership structure, and CEO compensation may impact firm innovation.
9:
第９回: Innovation in Family Firms
This session focuses on family-owned firms and their innovation.  The impact of family ownership and management on innovation is discussed.
10:
第１０回: Start-ups, Venture Firms, and Innovation
We discuss how entrepreneurial firms can engage in innovation including social innovation in and outside Japan..
11:
第１１回: Research Seminar
The instructor presents his own research and we then have a discussion.
12:
第１２回: Group Presentations
Each group makes a 15-minute presentation in class.
13:
第１３回: Wrap-up
We have an open discussion on selected topics.
14:
第１４回: Final Exam
Final exam is given during the final class.
reference: Class materials will be informed before each class session. .
grading_method: When attendance is more than two-thirds of the total class hours, the following components will be assessed.

name: Introduction to Mathematical Analysis ２ (Calculus)
summary_excerpt: The course provides the fundamentals of differential calculus of multivari......
summary: The course provides the fundamentals of differential calculus of multivariate functions and their optimization. First differential properties of univariate functions such as derivative rules, mean value theorem, Taylor's theorem and their optimization are explored. Then multivariate functions are introduced and their differential properties such as partial derivatives, gradients and Taylor's theorem are investigated. Finally, optimization with/without constraints are explored.

name: Modern Japanese Social and Cultural History
summary_excerpt: Since the development of modern Japanese society occurred at about the sam......
subtitle: A Cultural History of Salaryman in Modern and Contemporary Japan
summary:
Since the development of modern Japanese society occurred at about the same time as the development of schooling, the issue of "academic background" is often brought into focus, and in turn, the theme of the rise in hierarchy through the acquisition of academic credentials and the impatience to survive in modern society. In Japanese society, where "modernization" was synonymous with "westernization," the most popularized representation of "modernity" was to become a "salaryman," or "monthly wage earner. The combination of cultural aspects and salaryman may seem strange, but in fact, many "salarymen" (this term appeared in the mid-Taisho period) appear in modern Japanese culture such as literature. For example, in the works of Natsume Soseki, a representative of Meiji-era literature,
kotoyumin
(roughly transralted as "high-class or educatd idlers")
often appears, and this concept itself is inevitable with the emergence of a society in which "employment" was a prerequisite for the intellectual class. The term "salaryman" functions in Japanese society as a value that symbolizes  such as "stability" and "mediocre" rather than as a social class. People involved in establishing modern culture in Japan have expressed their resistance to and discomfort with such values in their works. This lecture will consider "salarymen" and their transformation mainly in literary works covering from the 1880s to the 1960s (from the Meiji 20s to the Mid-Showa period).
goal: By attending this lecture, students are expected to learn what kind of "social norms" were developed regarding the development of modernization in Japanese society and how they were reflected in cultural aspects. This lecture will refer to many literary works, so students will eventually be able to understand the history of Japanese literature as well.
study_outside:
Please read or at least follow the texts distributed a week in advance for the preparation. Most of the materials will be in Japanese, but the class will be conducted in English.
After the lecture, summarize what you learned and realized about the cultural aspects of Japanese society in a short essay. It will make the final report easy to write.
schedule_plan:
Week1: Course Orientation
Week2: Discourse around Salaryman
Week3: Agonizing Youth (Hanmon Seinen) and Meiji Japan
Week4: Love and Marriage in Futabatei Shimei's "Drifting Cloud"
Week5: Being Westernized, Being Established
Week6: Bourgeoise Utopia in Taisho Culture
Week7: Alienation and Pride: Cultural Representation of Salaryman in Mass Media
Week8: Difficulty of Being Husband and Wife in Kishida Kunio's "Paperbaloon"
Week9: Modernization and Great Kanto Earthquake
Week10: Love between Salaryman and Modern Girl
Week11: Profile of Salaryman Written in Literature in 1930s
Week12: Postwar Democracy and Love in Working Place
Week13: Unionization of Salaryman Culture
Week14: Birth of "Mr. Everyman" in 1960s
textbook: Nothing in particular.
reference:
Andrew Gordon, A History of Modern Japan: From Tokugawa Time to the Present.
Jordan Sand, House and Home in Modern Japan: Architecture, Domestic Space, and Bourgeois Culture, 1880-1930.
Miriam Silverberg, Erotic Grotesque Nonesense: The Mass Culture of Japanese Modern Times.

Participation: 20%

Report:60%

name: Business Administration
summary_excerpt: This course introduces the fundamental concepts and analytical frameworks ......
summary:
This course introduces the fundamental concepts and analytical frameworks of business administration. While business administration is a discipline that examines various phenomena related to corporations—encompassing diverse topics such as strategy, organization, entrepreneurship, technology, and internationalization—this course specifically focuses on strategic management.
Throughout the lectures, students will learn key concepts and analytical tools used in the field and explore their practical applications in real-world corporate settings. To deepen understanding, the course includes group work sessions where students will apply these frameworks to analyze specific case studies of Japanese companies.

Researching these events independently and connecting them to the theories discussed in class will significantly enhance your learning experience.

name: Aging and Public Policies in Contemporary Japanese Society
summary_excerpt: This course provides an overview of the major social challenges facing con......
subtitle: Aging and Public Policies in Contemporary Japanese Society
summary:
This course provides an overview of the major social challenges facing contemporary Japan. It examines how basic living standards can be ensured amid these challenges from the perspectives of
family
,
community
,
business and the market
, and
government
.
The course is organized into two main parts.
Part I
introduces key
theoretical lenses for understanding contemporary public policy
. Students will develop a foundational and critical understanding of what public policy is, how policies are formulated, and how social, economic, and political forces shape policy outcomes.
Part II
focuses on Japan’s aging society, exploring its historical, social, economic, and political contexts. Students will critically analyze the social welfare policies that have been implemented to respond to the needs of older adults, including pensions, healthcare, long-term care, and community-based support systems.
Throughout the course, we will examine both the challenges and opportunities posed by population aging in Japan. Particular attention will be paid to innovative and emerging approaches to aging and social welfare policy, with an emphasis on sustainability, social inclusion, and policy innovation.
goal: 1.Understand the historical, social, economic, and political context of Japan’s aging society.
study_outside:
1.Individual Interview & Mini-Midterm Poster: Conduct an interview with an older adult (aged 65 or above) in your home country to explore the social, economic, and/or community-level challenges they face. Based on the interview, prepare a mini-midterm report in the form of an academic poster (within one A4 sheet). The poster format is flexible.
2.Group Research Project and Presentation:Students will work in pairs (two students per group) to synthesize their individual interview findings and analyze them through the lens of public policies on aging and eldercare in Japan. Each group should identify key stakeholders and collaboratively design a policy solution. Groups must also specify which Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) are relevant to their case and policy proposal. Findings will be presented in an in-class presentation during the midterm period.
3.Final Policy Case Study Report:Building on the group discussion, presentation, and course materials, each student will independently write a policy case study based on their own interview case. The case study should demonstrate the application of concepts and analytical frameworks learned in this course. The final report must be at least 3,000 words in length, double-spaced, excluding references.
4.Documentary Reflection Papers:Students are required to watch selected documentaries addressing issues of aging and eldercare. After each viewing, students will submit a reflection paper linking the documentary content to key course themes, with particular attention to how aging policies are shaped by lived experiences and real-world contexts.

name: Climate Change and Sustainable Development
summary_excerpt: Climate change and sustainable development are critical global issues toda......
subtitle: Climate Change Economics
summary:
Climate change and sustainable development are critical global issues today. Given the intricate connections between the two, only well-designed and scientifically planned synergistic climate policy choices can significantly advance the achievement of sustainable development goals. To provide valuable information for decision-making at the community, local, national, and international levels, scientists and policymakers need to work together and adopt a comprehensive and holistic approach. In this course, students will be introduced to fundamental knowledge of climate change and the connections to sustainable development goals, and how to access the model and data to support decision making. This course will cover three main topics:
1.Demonstrate knowledge related to climate change and human societies, including the latest scientific consensus on climate change evidence from observations, impacts, driving factors, mitigation and adaptation, and carbon neutrality.
2.Explain the connection between climate change and sustainable development goals, emphasizing the nexus between climate change mitigation and high-quality economic development, energy security, food security, air pollution control, and human health improvement.
3.Practical sessions featuring case studies will provide hands-on experience with related models, including the latest IPCC CMIP6 climate models and Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSPs). These sessions will focus on understanding and predicting future climate change trends under various scenarios, considering both climate and socio-economic factors to support policy-making and strategic planning.

name: Family and Law I
summary_excerpt: In this course, we will examine the basic concepts and rules enshrined in ......
summary: In this course, we will examine the basic concepts and rules enshrined in laws related to "family" (in the case of Japan: the Civil Code, child welfare laws, DV Prevention Act, etc.), and explore the background of the enactment and subsequent reforms of these laws in Japan and elsewhere. We will also refer to important case law, where appropriate. The lecturer will also introduce relevant concepts and ideas from other disciplines, such as sociology and child psychology, to facilitate inter-disciplinary learning and thinking. While learning about the legal framework surrounding partner relationships (marriage and other), parent-child relationships, and so forth, we will consider the statutory rules in the context of various issues that families (and their individual members) face in modern society, such as child poverty, aging societies, feminization of poverty, etc.
goal:
・The students will learn the fundamentals of law concerning families, particularly spouses, partners, and parent-child relationships.
・The students will be able to explain how the law seeks to address various social issues surrounding families and individual family members, and why it sometimes fails to do so effectively.
・The students will develop the ability to discuss what improvements and developments are needed in systems (including legal systems) to resolve various social issues concerning families and their members, building on an accurately understanding of current law and referencing knowledge in related legal fields.
study_outside: Students are expected to spend on average 120 minutes on preparation for each class, by reviewing assigned materials. Students are also expected to spend on average 120 minutes on reviewing class material after each class.
schedule_plan:
【第1回】 Introduction to Family Law
We will do an introduction into law in general and family law in particular.
【第2回】 Formal Marriage – How and Why?
We will learn about the legal requirements and conditions of formal marriage (including marriage between persons of the same sex/gender) in various jurisdictions.
【第3回】 Diverse Partner-Relationships – Options, Merits, Shortcomings
We will learn about adult relationships outside of formal marriage (de facto relationships, civil partnerships, etc.).
【第4回】 Parents and Children (1)
We will learn about who is considered to be a “legal parent” and why. Key words: biological parents, intended parents, legal parents, children “born out of wedlock”, adoption.
【第5回】 Parents and Children (2)
We will learn about medically assisted reproduction and legal parentage, as well as the child’s right to know their origins.
【第6回】Parents and Children (3)
We will learn about when the (birth) parents cannot raise their children. Key words: foster parents, adoption, anonymous birth.
【第7回】Parents and Children (4)
We will continue learning about when the (birth) parents cannot raise their children. Key words: foster parents, adoption, anonymous birth.
【第8回】Parents and Children (5)
We will learn about children in so-called “rainbow families” (families with sexual minority parents).
【第9回】 Divorce (1)
We will learn about the legal regulation of relationship-breakup, the required formalities (including the Japanese divorce by consent), the financial consequences, the implications of gender, and so forth.
【第10回】Divorce (2)
We will continue to learn about the legal regulation of relationship-breakup, the required formalities (including the Japanese divorce by consent), the financial consequences, the implications of gender, and so forth.
【第11回】 Divorce (3)
We will learn about divorce and children (custody/parenting time, contact/access)
【第12回】 Divorce (4)
We will learn about poverty in single-parent households and child support.
【第13回】 Court Proceedings and the Child’s Right to be Heard
We will learn about the extent to which and how a child’s right to be heard in family court proceedings is guaranteed.
【第１4回】 Gender and Family Law
We will learn about how gender stereotypes and gender biases appear and play out in family law and the court.

name: Global Lab 4: Field Inquiry (Vietnam – Adaptive Peace)
teacher: 早田　宰／グエン　ホアン　タン　ザイン
summary_excerpt: This course constitutes the fieldwork component of the Global Lab program ......
subtitle: Living Systems, Adaptive Peace, and Community Knowledge in Northern Vietnam
summary:
This course constitutes the fieldwork component of the Global Lab program and explores the concept of Adaptive Peace through field-based learning in the mountainous regions of northern Vietnam. Although completion of
Global Lab 1
is not a formal prerequisite, students are expected to have acquired fundamental social research skills prior to enrollment. Students are also strongly encouraged to take
Global Lab 5: Synthesis, Analysis & Report
to deepen their learning through post-fieldwork reflection and analysis. During the program, students will visit highland communities in Da Bac District, Hoa Binh Province, near Hanoi. The fieldwork focuses on Dao ethnic minority communities, where students will observe local lifestyles, ecological environments, social relations, economic activities, value systems, and knowledge practices. A key challenge in contemporary society is balancing globalization with the sustainability and harmony of local communities. At the same time, the concept of societal wealth has expanded beyond financial capital to include natural, human, social, and knowledge capital. Vietnam’s diplomatic strategy, often described as “Bamboo Diplomacy,” emphasizes flexibility and balance in international relations. Similar forms of adaptive flexibility can also be observed at the community level. In recent years, some highland communities have begun developing community-based tourism (CBT) and new forms of engagement with external societies. The course examines the role of local governance, including women-centered community coordination groups, as well as collaboration between district authorities and local communities to promote transparent and participatory governance. It also considers the role of international NGOs such as Action for Poverty (AFAP) in providing intermediary support and capacity building. The course further addresses challenges associated with CBT development, including equity among ethnic groups—particularly between Muong and Dao communities—and environmental conservation. Using case study methods, students conduct field observations, interviews, and participant observation to examine local practices and reflect on broader questions related to international development and social innovation. A small student team will produce a web-based case report introducing the experience of Sung Hamlet. Findings will be further analyzed in Global Lab 5, where they will be developed into structured social scientific knowledge.
※The fieldwork is planned from November 4 (Wed) to November 8 (Sun), 2026
[Note]
This course requires a selection process based on a statement of purpose. Please be sure to submit your statement of purpose during the course registration period (approximately 100–200 words in English, or 300–400 characters in Japanese).
Please check the following link for instructions on how to submit the statement of purpose.
02_Course Registration Procedure – Support Anywhere
＞TIPS3: Courses that Require a Reason for Application
goal: ・To understand everyday life, ecological environments, social relations, economic activities, forms of exchange and diplomacy, value systems, and knowledge practices through fieldwork, as well as the relationships among these elements and their changes over time.
study_outside: Field Methods
schedule_plan:
Fieldwork Schedule
(Tentative)
November 4 – Arrival in Hanoi
Students arrive in Hanoi, Vietnam. An introductory lecture will be given by Prof. Hong Minh on “Changing Rural Vietnam.” The day will also include a short field orientation and an exploratory visit to selected sites in Hanoi to provide historical and social context for the fieldwork.
November 5 – Transfer to Hoa Binh Province
Students depart Hanoi by chartered bus and will join student groups from Vietnam Japan University and a partner university in Germany (tentative). In the morning, the group will visit the Vietnam Military History Museum to understand Vietnam’s historical background. After lunch near the museum, the group will travel to Hoa Binh Province and arrive at a Dao ethnic minority village. Upon arrival, the community will provide a guided walk through the village and introduce their community-based tourism initiatives. Students will conduct initial observation and site familiarization. Overnight stay at a village guesthouse.
November 6 – Group Field Research
Students will conduct group-based fieldwork and interviews according to selected thematic topics, such as:
・Food culture and ancient tea trees
・Chinese influence, Daoism, and the Chu Nom script
・Gender roles and women’s work (e.g., dyeing workshops, paper-making)
・Local aspirations, peace, and community solidarity
In the evening, students will hold a group discussion session reflecting on their findings and planning the outputs of the action research component. Students will begin developing ideas for post-fieldwork products such as web content, introduction pages, or informational leaflets about the community. Overnight stay at the village guesthouse.
November 7 – Student Presentations and Return to Hanoi
Students present their preliminary findings and discuss ideas for future collaboration with the village, including possible online collaboration after returning to Japan. After the session, the group travels back to Hanoi. Overnight stay in Hanoi.
November 8 – Reflection and Departure
Final reflection session and discussion of key insights from the fieldwork. Students then depart for Tokyo.

name: Global Lab 5: Synthesis, Analysis & Report
teacher: 早田　宰／堀　芳枝／北村　能寛／山田　寛邦
summary_excerpt: This course constitutes the final stage of the Global Lab program and aims......
subtitle: From Field Experience to Social Knowledge
summary: This course constitutes the final stage of the Global Lab program and aims to synthesize and analyze the experiences and insights obtained through fieldwork conducted in locations such as Hong Kong, Taiwan, and Vietnam from a social science perspective. During the fieldwork phase, students observe local initiatives and social challenges directly in the field and engage in dialogue with diverse community members. In this course, these experiences are not treated merely as personal impressions but are systematically analyzed through an iterative process between theory and practice, transforming them into structured social knowledge. In the analytical process, particular attention will be given to the various resources and relationships that constitute regional societies, including natural environments, human networks, cultural contexts, human resources, institutional frameworks, and economic activities. Students will explore how these elements interact to shape regional societies. The course will also examine how recent digital technologies and emerging social initiatives are being utilized to address local challenges. Furthermore, the course promotes dialogue on diverse social experiences across Asia. By sharing perspectives rooted in different historical and cultural contexts, students will develop the ability to connect global perspectives with local realities when understanding social issues. The first half of the course will be delivered in an on-demand format focusing on theoretical perspectives and analytical methods. The latter half will consist of intensive sessions including workshops and discussions. Ultimately, students will compile the knowledge gained through the Global Lab into reports and presentations. These outputs will serve as knowledge resources that can be shared with future students and community partners. The course aims to help students understand the process of knowledge generation, in which field experiences are transformed into social knowledge.
goal:
・Organize fieldwork experiences and explain them from a social science perspective.
・Understand the diverse resources and relationships that constitute regional societies.
・Analyze social issues through comparative perspectives across different regions and cultures.
・Envision the potential and future trajectories of regional societies based on a grounded understanding of current conditions.
・Present field-based knowledge in the form of reports and presentations.
study_outside:
Before the Session:
Students will review a large number of case study reports and booklets available on the web (PDF format). They will analyze how these reports are structured, focusing on the following aspects: ・overall organization and structure, research objectives and intended messages, the use of key diagrams, clarity of arguments, coherence and consistency, readability and layout design etc.
After the Session:
Students will transform their field experiences into social knowledge and take responsibility for the stewardship of their work. Final outputs will be archived and shared by uploading them to a designated BOX link, where they will be stored as knowledge resources for future students and community partners.

An overview of how fieldwork experiences can be organized and interpreted as academic knowledge.
2:

Review and organize research experiences in Hong Kong, Taiwan, and Vietnam.
3:
Integrating Field Experiences (1) (On-demand) – Nguyen
Identifying shared themes and issues through cross-regional comparison.
4:
Field-Based Interdisciplinary Knowledge (On-demand) – Yamada
Learning perspectives that connect field experiences, academic disciplines, and local practical knowledge.
5:
Organizing and Analyzing Field Data (On-demand) – Kitamura
Interpreting relationships among people, institutions, culture, and resources in regional societies.
6:
Understanding the Structure of Social Issues (On-demand) – Nguyen & Yamada
Interpreting relationships among people, institutions, culture, and resources in regional societies.
7:
Structuring Research Outcomes –October 24 (Sat) Period 2- Soda & Kitamura
Learning how to structure research findings and prepare presentations.
8:
Knowledge Sharing and Archiving -October 24 (Sat) Period 3 – Soda
Methods for organizing research outcomes so that they can be shared with future students and researchers.
9:
Introduction to Output Formats -October 24 (Sat) Period 4 – Soda & Yamada
Students will explore different formats for presenting research results and determine the format for each group.
Examples: Research Paper, Policy Brief, Field Report, Visual Story, Digital Archive, Community Feedback Report
10:
Regional Comparison and Issue Analysis -October 31 (Sat) Period 3 – Yamada
Students will determine the design of their Key Diagram and discuss it within their groups. In this course, visualization plays an important role in organizing field-based knowledge. Each group is required to produce one Key Diagram as part of the final output. A Key Diagram is a visual representation that summarizes the structure and relationships within a regional society and serves as the central conceptual diagram of the research.
Examples: Social system diagram, Issue structure diagram, Regional resource map, Process diagram, Future scenario diagram
11:
Structural Analysis of Social Issues (Discussion)-November 21 (Sat) Period 2 – Nguyen
Discussion on the structural analysis of social issues based on each group’s Key Diagram.
12:
Workshop on Organizing Research Outcomes -November 21 (Sat) Period 3 – Kitamura
Workshop on organizing research outcomes and refining the structure of the final outputs.
13:
Student Presentations (Internal Presentation) -December 12 (Sat) Period 2 – All
Final Presentation (Video-recorded student presentations)
14:
Course Summary and Future Research Directions Nguyen & Soda
Online Reporting Session with Community Partners
Mid-January (On-demand publication) Community Feedback Session: Sharing results with partners in Hong Kong, Taiwan, Vietnam, and other regional collaborators.
